food_subgroup: Japanese set meal (teishoku) with grilled fish, rice, miso soup, and side dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for miso grilled mackerel set meal; closest parent is mackerel food product (FOODON_03301116).
ontology.foodex2_description: Atlantic mackerel and related species, grilled
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers grilled mackerel; no specific code for miso-marinated preparation or full teishoku set. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for miso grilled mackerel set meal.

serving.description: 1 set meal (approximately 550–650 g including rice, fish, miso soup, and sides)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Japanese teishoku set consisting of one grilled miso mackerel fillet (approximately 120–150 g), one bowl of steamed white rice (approximately 200 g), one bowl of miso soup (approximately 200 ml), pickled vegetables, and a small side dish, totalling approximately 600 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central: Mackerel, Atlantic, cooked, dry heat (NDB 15047) and Japanese Teishoku composite estimation; supplemented with USDA SR Legacy entries for steamed white rice, miso soup, and side components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a complete miso grilled saba teishoku set meal. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite of: (1) USDA FoodData Central grilled Atlantic mackerel (NDB 15047) for the fish component; (2) USDA SR Legacy cooked white rice for the rice component; (3) USDA FoodData Central miso soup for the soup component; (4) standard Japanese pickled vegetable and tamagoyaki values from USDA SR Legacy. Mackerel component weighted at approximately 25% of total set weight, rice at approximately 33%, miso soup at approximately 33%, and sides at approximately 9%. Per-100g values represent the blended composite of the full set meal. Miso marinade contributes additional sodium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 (Atkinson, Foster-Powell, Brand-Miller); University of Sydney GI Database — white rice (Japanese short-grain, cooked) GI ~54–56; composite set meal GI estimated from rice-dominant carbohydrate fraction
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a complete miso grilled saba teishoku set. The glycemic response of this set meal is dominated by the steamed Japanese short-grain white rice component. University of Sydney GI Database lists Japanese short-grain white rice at GI 54–56 (mean ~54). The fish, miso soup, and vegetable components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates. Composite GI estimated at 54 (low category, ≤55). Glycemic load per 600 g serving calculated as: (13.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 600 g / 100) × 54 / 100 = 81 g total carbohydrate × 0.54 ≈ 43.7; rounded to 39.0 using net available carbohydrate after fibre adjustment (net carb ~12.0 g/100g × 6 × 0.54 = 38.9 ≈ 39.0). gi_category set to low per validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from miso paste marinade and miso soup
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with this set meal as the miso marinade and miso soup together contribute approximately 1,860 mg sodium per serving (600 g), which represents approximately 81% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg; requesting reduced-sodium miso or limiting soup consumption is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from steamed white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The steamed white rice in this set meal provides the primary glycemic carbohydrate load (GI ~54, GL ~39 per serving); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of the rice component and consider substituting with brown rice or reducing the rice portion to manage postprandial blood glucose, while the high-protein mackerel and miso soup components help moderate the overall glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from mackerel offset by high sodium from miso
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mackerel (saba) is an excellent source of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are cardioprotective and associated with reduced triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; however, the high sodium content from miso marinade and miso soup warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and limiting miso soup or requesting low-sodium preparation is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Miso Grilled Mackerel Set Meal (味噌焼きサバ定食, Misoyaki Saba Teishoku) is a traditional Japanese teishoku (set meal) consisting of a grilled mackerel fillet marinated in miso paste, served alongside a bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, a bowl of miso soup, pickled vegetables (tsukemono), and often a small side dish such as tamagoyaki (Japanese rolled omelette) or simmered tofu. The mackerel (saba) is marinated in a mixture of white or red miso paste, mirin, and sake, then grilled until the surface is caramelised and slightly charred, yielding a deeply savoury, umami-rich flavour with a tender, flaky interior and a lightly crisp skin. The miso marinade imparts a characteristic sweet-salty depth that complements the naturally rich, oily flesh of the mackerel. This dish is a staple of Japanese home cooking and restaurant menus, particularly in teishoku-style eateries and izakayas. Nutritionally, the set meal provides a balanced macronutrient profile: the mackerel contributes high-quality protein and significant omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are associated with cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits; the rice provides the primary carbohydrate energy source; and the miso soup adds probiotics and micronutrients including B vitamins and minerals. The primary nutritional concern is the high sodium content derived from miso paste and miso soup, which may be problematic for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the set is estimated at approximately 54 (low category), driven by the Japanese short-grain rice component. This dish is suitable for most healthy adults as part of a balanced diet and is a good source of vitamin B12, vitamin D, selenium, and phosphorus from the mackerel component. In Singapore, this dish is served at Japanese restaurants such as GyoGyo at Funan Mall.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a complete miso grilled saba teishoku set meal. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central and USDA SR Legacy entries for the individual components (grilled mackerel, steamed white rice, miso soup, pickled vegetables, tamagoyaki). The mackerel component is well-supported by USDA data. The GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database data for Japanese short-grain white rice as the dominant glycemic component. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and variability in restaurant portion sizes and miso sodium content.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates for the full set meal per 100 g of total set weight, based on USDA FoodData Central and USDA SR Legacy component data. Individual restaurant preparations will vary, particularly in sodium content from miso marinade concentration and miso soup recipe. 2. Sodium content is estimated conservatively at 310 mg/100 g (approximately 1,860 mg per 600 g serving); actual sodium may be higher depending on miso paste type and quantity used. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as mackerel and rice-based components contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; any trace amounts are below detection threshold. 4. GI is estimated at 54 (low, ≤55) based on Japanese short-grain white rice GI from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for the complete set meal exists. gi_category set to low per validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low). 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.2), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.1), potassium_mg (210.0), calcium_mg (28.0), iron_mg (0.7), cholesterol_mg (38.0), vitamin_c_mg (1.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (18.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.08), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.12), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (2.8), vitamin_b6_mg (0.22), vitamin_b12_mcg (3.5), folate_mcg_dfe (12.0), vitamin_d_mcg (2.1), vitamin_e_mg (0.6), phosphorus_mg (145.0), magnesium_mg (22.0), zinc_mg (0.7), selenium_mcg (18.0), water_g (72.0) are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite component data and are not individually confirmed from a single source entry. 6. The food_id GD004169 and restaurant attribution to GyoGyo - Funan are preserved from the input seed record. 7. This record is cuisine category Japanese; mandatory multilingual field is Japanese native script (味噌焼きサバ定食), which is present.
